query: complete bilateral palsy of recurrent laryngeal nerves symptoms

retrieved_docs.title: Parathyroidectomy -- Complications -- Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury is one of the most feared complications of parathyroid surgery. Injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve results in paresis or palsy of the vocal cord, causing hoarseness (unilateral damage) or stridor, airway occlusion (bilateral damage), and an increased risk of aspiration, may need immediate reintubation or occasionally tracheostomy. Most recurrent laryngeal nerve injuries are transient. Approximately 1.1% of the patients presented permanent postoperative vocal cord paresis after thyroid and parathyroid surgery due to recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. Injuries after parathyroidectomy are less frequent compared to thyroid surgery. Most patients with transient postoperative recurrent laryngeal nerve injury recovered normal vocal cord mobility within 6 months. [31] Identifying the recurrent laryngeal nerve during thyroid dissection is the gold standard to avoid nerve injury. [32] Intraoperative nerve monitoring during parathyroidectomy is a promising adjunct to visualization alone in detecting nerve structures during neck dissection, which may decrease the likelihood of recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. [33] A reinnervation procedure should be attempted When recurrent laryngeal nerve transection is recognized during parathyroidectomy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Bilateral Vocal Cord Paralysis -- Etiology -- Causes of bilateral vocal cord paresis:
retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic causes: In addition to prolonged intubation, surgical trauma to the vagus or recurrent laryngeal nerves can weaken the contraction of the intrinsic laryngeal muscles resulting in impaired vocal cord function. This is classically seen in the thyroid, tracheal and esophageal resections. This can remain as a temporary paresis or can progress to true paralysis as the nerves undergo Wallerian degeneration.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve has the potential to cause unilateral vocal cord paralysis. Patients with this typically complain of new-onset hoarseness, changes in vocal pitch, or noisy breathing. Bilateral vocal cord paralysis is much less common due to both left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves being affected but can present with more serious symptoms, including significant difficulties breathing and swallowing. Recent surgeries of the head/neck or recent intubation should prompt suspicion of possible injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Signs of underlying malignancy should also be investigated, such as hemoptysis, severe coughing, unexplained weight loss, tobacco/alcohol use, or dysphagia. Less common causes, including recent viral illness or neck trauma, may also point towards an underlying RLN injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential for recurrent laryngeal nerve injury with resulting vocal cord paralysis can include: Iatrogenic – Endotracheal intubation or during surgical procedures of the skull base, neck, or chest Malignancy – Especially of the skull base, neck, or chest Trauma – Including the neck and chest in addition to the larynx Neurological – Including stroke (specifically lateral medullary syndrome), bulbar palsies, and demyelinating diseases Idiopathic – Diagnosis of exclusion

retrieved_docs.title: Bilateral Vocal Cord Paralysis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Benninger et al estimated that bilateral vocal cord paralysis can be attributed to surgical trauma in 44% of cases, malignancies in 17%, secondary to endotracheal intubation in 15% of cases, due to neurologic disease in 12% of patients and idiopathic causes in 12% of cases. [2] Following thyroid or other surgery, a bilateral vocal cord paralysis can result in acute respiratory distress necessitating urgent airway intervention. Damage to the RLN adversely affects both abduction and adduction of the vocal cords due to laryngeal muscle denervation. Since the adductor muscle fibers are four times greater than abductor muscle fibers, the vocal cords assume a static paramedian position following injury. [3] Direct stimulation of the vocal cords by an ETT and cuff during intubation and extubation may lead to vocal cord edema. Case reports have also described injury by direct surgical trauma, nerve division or ligature, pressure-induced neuropraxia, and postoperative edema. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: One of the most serious complications is respiratory distress occurring from bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. However, this is rare, as unilateral RLN injury is much more common. Aspiration with resulting aspiration pneumonia is a substantial complication to consider in susceptible patients. In addition, the resulting dysphagia or voice changes from unilateral injury can still pose substantial morbidity to patients and affect the overall quality of life. This is especially true for patients who have a career based on the use of their vocal cords, such as speakers or singers.

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In another article reviewing 2,267 cases of unilateral vocal cord palsy, the top three causes were surgery, cancer, and idiopathic accounting for 36.9%, 29.7%, and 20.9% of cases, respectively. [8] Therefore recurrent laryngeal nerve injury and unilateral vocal cord palsy seem to have similar etiologies, as expected.

retrieved_docs.title: Unilateral Vocal Fold Paralysis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal reinnervation utilizes functioning nerves in the RLN's vicinity to reestablish laryngeal tone and movement. The ansa cervicalis, phrenic, and hypoglossal nerves have all been used as nerve pedicles with good voice outcomes [69] .

retrieved_docs.title: Bilateral Vocal Cord Paralysis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the various etiologies of bilateral vocal cord paralysis. Describe the evaluation of bilateral vocal cord paralysis. Outline the management options available for bilateral vocal cord paralysis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Larynx Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve -- Surgical Considerations -- Nerve Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring the RLN and SLN is essential during neck procedures, such as thyroid lobectomy and thyroidectomy. [17] An indirect laryngoscopy serves as a crucial tool for assessing RLN integrity both before and after surgery. In cases of vocal fold dysfunction, reinnervation techniques may be applied to the abductors and tensors using a nerve-muscle pedicle. However, this approach is only viable if the vocal fold is not fixed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Muscles -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic causes of injury to the vagus nerve and its branches can lead to a unilateral paresis or paralysis of the vocal fold. Recurrent laryngeal nerve damage can be a complication in operations such as thyroid surgery where unilateral damage leads to hoarseness or swallowing difficulties. Damage to the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve can lead to an inability to increase pitch as the cricothyroid is unable to lengthen the vocal folds. Bilateral damage to the nerves, while extremely uncommon, can have severe sequelae of respiratory distress (secondary to failure of abduction and resultant midline position of the vocal folds) and occasionally can lead to a tracheostomy. [7] [8] [5] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Given the coursing nature of the recurrent laryngeal nerve, there are many injuries that can cause pathology, and damage to the nerve anywhere along its path that can cause impaired vocal function. One of the more commonly cited injuries is secondary to surgical intervention. One study reviewing over 800 patients showed that surgical intervention was the most important cause. [3] This can include any surgical interventions of the chest, neck, or skull base. However, the most frequently studied interventions are thyroidectomies and parathyroidectomies. One study showed that permanent, operation-related vocal cord palsies from injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve had an incidence of approximately 1%. They also reported the incidence of RLN injuries after thyroid surgery and parathyroid surgery to be 14% and 7%, respectively. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In an article reviewing multiple studies on unilateral RLN injury, surgery is cited as the most common cause with most studies putting it as the cause of 30 to 40% of all RLN injuries. Tumors are the second leading cause accounting for approximately 17% to 32% of injuries. Idiopathic causes are cited around 10% to 27% of all RLN injuries with endotracheal intubations last at around 7% to 11% of all causes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Unilateral Vocal Cord Paralysis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic injury has traditionally been attributed to thyroidectomy operations, the likelihood of which increases when there is aberrant anatomy of the recurrent laryngeal nerve. [3] Intra-operative nerve stimulators are frequently used to avoid injury, but their effectiveness has not been clinically proven. [4] [5] Other surgeries that lead to injury of the recurrent laryngeal nerve include anterior cervical spine surgery, oesophagectomy, and cardiothoracic surgery, although any procedure during which an endotracheal tube can put prolonged pressure on the nerve may result in paralysis. Traumatic UVCP is common but not well understood, with proposed mechanisms of injury including direct recurrent laryngeal nerve trauma and arytenoid dislocation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Bilateral Vocal Cord Paralysis -- Etiology -- Causes of bilateral vocal cord paralysis:
retrieved_docs.content: Malignancy: Advanced laryngeal malignancy can destroy the vocal cords or their neuromuscular controls, leading to paralysis. Additionally, subglottic, tracheal, esophageal, and thyroid malignancies can extend and involve the RLNs, leading to bilateral vocal cord paralysis.

retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia and hemiparesis are clinical conditions that fall under the broader category of paralysis; they involve loss of motor function on one side of the body, with hemiplegia denoting complete weakness and hemiparesis indicating partial weakness. Recognizing these disorders as subtypes of paralysis helps clinicians diagnose the extent of neurological impairment and tailor rehabilitation and treatment strategies accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: palsy, combined myasthenic‑Bell’s palsy, and sciatic nerve lesion, all associated with motor weakness or paralysis. Muscular and neuromuscular disorders (muscle disorder, muscular neuropathy, broader neuromuscular disease) are also classified under this category, linking muscle pathology to peripheral nerve involvement and secondary paralysis. Ancillary entries such as acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) and disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are connected to muscle disorder and secondary paralysis, highlighting the overlap between neuropathic and adjacent musculoskeletal inflammation. Overall, the taxonomy connects genetic, metabolic, toxic, inflammatory, traumatic, and compressive insults to peripheral and cranial nerves, with clinical outcomes of pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: process of unknown origin that also targets these nerves. Malignant‑associated polyneuropathy and vascular‑collagen‑related polyneuropathy illustrate the susceptibility of peripheral nerves to systemic malignancy and autoimmune vasculitis, respectively. Severe neuropathy underscores the potential for profound functional impairment when peripheral nerve damage is extensive. Acute infectious bronchitis can extend to peripheral nerves, and specific inflammatory nerve lesions—mononeuritis and its multifocal form—directly affect individual or multiple peripheral nerve branches. Collectively, these relationships highlight that a wide spectrum of metabolic, toxic, idiopathic, neoplastic, autoimmune, infectious and inflammatory etiologies converge on the peripheral nervous system. Recognizing peripheral nerve involvement is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, monitoring and comprehensive patient care across the diverse disorders classified under Other disorders of the

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea serves as the anatomical site for tracheostomy procedures, and these procedures may be performed as either temporary or permanent interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of distinct neurological conditions. It is classified as تكزز, as اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), as اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني (multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease), as التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), as التهاب العصب الأحادي (mononeuritis), as الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic atrophy of the lower limb), as داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and as وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and Bell’s palsy). By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system, clinicians can recognize that they share a common classification despite differing pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and management strategies, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: strategies in the management of neuromuscular, neuro‑vascular and entrapment neuropathies, supporting precise diagnosis, coding and coordinated patient care across overlapping neurologic, sensory and musculoskeletal domains.

retrieved_docs.content: upper facial atrophy (“ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي”) as a child condition, illustrating how cranial nerve pathology can produce both motor deficits and characteristic facial changes. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate a hierarchical pattern in which broad neuropathic and musculoskeletal categories encompass specific nerve lesions and syndromes, all of which may converge on the clinical outcome of paralysis. Recognizing both the broader disease contexts (such as peripheral neuropathy and carpal tunnel syndrome) and the narrower, nerve‑specific lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and prevention of motor impairment in patient care.
